data.title: Alphabet Talks Put Marvell Closer To Core Of AI Infrastructure
data.description:
• Alphabet is reported to be in advanced talks with Marvell Technology to co develop two custom AI chips.
  • The discussions focus on hardware for AI inference and memory processing that could support Alphabet's growing AI cloud services.
  • The potential partnership would deepen Marvell's role within the global AI supply chain alongside major hyperscale customers.

For investors tracking Marvell Technology, ticker NasdaqGS:MRVL, the talks with Alphabet mark a fresh development that goes beyond recent attention on the share price. The stock is at $147.84, with returns of 12.6% over the past week, 68.2% over the past month, and 65.4% year to date. Over longer periods, NasdaqGS:MRVL shows returns of 200.2% over 1 year, 285.3% over 3 years, and a very large gain over 5 years.

If Alphabet and Marvell formalize an agreement, it could influence how you think about Marvell's role in AI infrastructure and its customer mix. The focus on custom inference and memory oriented silicon also highlights where future product and capital allocation decisions might concentrate, especially as large cloud providers reassess their reliance on existing chip suppliers.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Marvell Technology by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Marvell Technology.

2 things going right for Marvell Technology that this headline doesn't cover.

The prospect of Alphabet partnering with Marvell on two custom AI chips puts Marvell closer to the center of hyperscale AI build outs. A memory processing unit coupled with a new tensor processing unit for inference fits directly with Marvell’s focus on custom data center silicon and interconnect, an area already drawing investor attention through earlier work with companies like NVIDIA and large cloud providers. If talks progress into a concrete design win, Marvell could deepen its role inside Google’s AI stack, similar to how Broadcom and Nvidia supply key pieces of data center hardware today. At the same time, the news underlines a key tension for investors. Analysts have already highlighted that Marvell’s revenue is concentrated in data center customers and large custom projects, so another hyperscaler partnership could add both opportunity and contract specific risk. Short term share price moves around the headlines, including reactions in Broadcom’s stock, also show how closely the market watches supplier shifts in AI hardware.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Marvell Technology Narrative

  • The potential Alphabet collaboration lines up with the narrative that Marvell is gaining more custom data center silicon wins and deeper exposure to AI and cloud infrastructure spending.
  • It could also intensify the concentration on a small group of hyperscale customers, which the narrative already flags as a source of volatility if orders change or in house chip efforts accelerate.
  • The specific focus on a memory processing unit paired with an inference TPU is not explicitly covered in the narrative, so the implications for mix between interconnect, compute and optics may not be fully reflected.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Marvell Technology to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Higher dependency on large cloud customers like Alphabet, especially when combined with existing exposure to other hyperscalers, can increase earnings volatility if any one customer changes direction.
  • ⚠️ Custom AI chip projects often require heavy R&D and tight supply coordination, so delays in design sign off, testing or production could weigh on profitability in certain periods.
  • 🎁 Progress on co developed AI chips would support the view that Marvell is a key supplier for next generation data center infrastructure, alongside peers such as Broadcom and Nvidia.
  • 🎁 A deeper role in Alphabet’s AI hardware stack could reinforce Marvell’s position in high value data center silicon and help support its broader efforts in networking and accelerator attach solutions.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on whether the talks with Alphabet lead to a formal design win, clear timelines for tape out and production, and any commentary from Marvell’s management at events like the ISIG Symposium or future earnings calls. It also helps to track how this potential work fits alongside existing partnerships, including with NVIDIA, and whether analysts begin to adjust expectations for data center revenue or contract concentration. Any updates from Alphabet on its TPU roadmap, and on how it balances internal efforts with suppliers such as Marvell, Broadcom and Nvidia, will give further clues about how durable Marvell’s role in AI hardware might be.

data.title: SK Hynix Targets AI Server Growth With SOCAMM2 And Semidynamics Move
data.description:
• SK hynix has begun mass production of its SOCAMM2 next generation memory module for AI servers.
  • The SOCAMM2 is designed to improve performance and efficiency for large language model training and inference workloads.
  • SK hynix has also invested in Semidynamics to advance memory centric AI infrastructure development.

SK hynix (KOSE:A000660), currently trading at around ₩1,166,000, is positioned in the AI hardware story with this new SOCAMM2 rollout. The stock has seen recent moves, with returns of 12.1% over the past week, 15.8% over 30 days and 72.2% year to date, placing it among the more aggressively performing names in the AI related memory space.

For investors tracking AI infrastructure, this combination of mass produced AI focused memory and closer work with Semidynamics highlights a company engaging with the technical demands of large language models. The scale of its 1 year and 3 year returns, which are described as very large, reflects how closely market attention has linked SK hynix to AI memory trends, and this latest step keeps that connection in focus.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for SK hynix by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on SK hynix.

📰 Beyond the headline: 0 risks and 4 things going right for SK hynix that every investor should see.

The SOCAMM2 rollout positions SK hynix more squarely in the core of AI server spend, where memory bandwidth and power use directly affect the cost of running large language models. By adapting smartphone style low power LPDDR5X DRAM for servers, SK hynix is targeting data center operators that want higher capacity and lower energy use in the same rack footprint. The company highlights more than double the bandwidth and over 75% better power efficiency versus conventional RDIMM, which speaks directly to AI providers focused on performance per watt and cost per token. Design alignment with NVIDIA’s Vera Rubin platform also links SK hynix to one of the key GPU ecosystems, an area where peers like Samsung Electronics and Micron are also competing for attach rate in AI servers. The investment in Semidynamics, which is architecting processors around memory constraints rather than pure compute, reinforces this memory centric angle and could help SK hynix influence how future AI chips are designed to use its modules more efficiently.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The SK hynix Narrative

  • The mass production of SOCAMM2 directly feeds into the narrative that high performance AI memory can support premium positioning in next generation DRAM and LPDDR server modules.
  • The heavy focus on AI servers could heighten exposure to swings in AI infrastructure spending, which is one of the concerns in the narrative around high investment needs and competition.
  • The Semidynamics partnership, built around a memory centric processor architecture and 3 nm progress, adds a processor angle that may not be fully captured in a memory only storyline.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for SK hynix to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Concentration in AI focused memory, including SOCAMM2 and HBM, increases sensitivity to AI server demand cycles and potential delays in customer deployment plans.
  • ⚠️ The need to support advanced nodes, new packaging, and memory centric processor partnerships could keep capital expenditure and R&D requirements high for an extended period.
  • 🎁 SOCAMM2’s bandwidth and power efficiency profile, plus alignment with NVIDIA platforms, may strengthen SK hynix’s position in AI data centers versus global memory peers.
  • 🎁 The Semidynamics investment gives SK hynix a seat at the table in co designing processors and memory, which can improve attach opportunities as AI models demand larger context windows.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it makes sense to watch how quickly SOCAMM2 is adopted across major cloud service providers, and whether it becomes a standard option in AI server configurations built around GPUs from NVIDIA and others. Investors can also track how SK hynix talks about capacity allocation between SOCAMM2, HBM, and other DRAM products, as this will shape its mix of AI versus non AI revenue. Updates on joint work with Semidynamics, especially around rack level systems or reference designs, may indicate how deeply SK hynix is integrating memory into full AI infrastructure stacks.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Continental (XTRA:CON) After A 40% One Year Gain?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Continental is attractively priced today or already reflects most of its potential, this article walks through what the current share price might indicate about value.
  • The stock last closed at €66.68, with returns of 4.8% over 7 days, 15.4% over 30 days, a 2.8% decline year to date, and 40.8% over 1 year. This gives you a sense of both recent momentum and the risks of buying after a strong run.
  • Recent coverage around Continental has focused on its position in the auto components industry and how investors are weighing that against broader sector conditions. These headlines provide useful context for the recent share price moves, even if they do not change the underlying long term value drivers.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Continental currently scores 2 out of 6. The next sections will walk through what that means across different valuation methods and then finish with a way to pull all of those signals together in a more complete view of value.

Continental scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Continental Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model looks at the cash Continental is expected to generate in the future and discounts those projected cash flows back to today to estimate what the whole business might be worth in €.

For Continental, the latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of about €643.3m. Analysts and model estimates then project free cash flow turning positive and reaching €1.52b by 2030. The ten year path between now and 2035 is built from a mix of analyst forecasts up to 2029 and Simply Wall St extrapolations after that, all still in € and all discounted back using the 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model.

Putting those cash flows together gives an estimated intrinsic value of €128.40 per share. Compared to the recent share price of €66.68, this DCF suggests Continental trades at roughly a 48.1% discount, which on this model alone indicates that the shares may be undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Continental is undervalued by 48.1%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 229 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Continental.

Approach 2: Continental Price vs Sales

Price based on sales is often useful for companies where earnings are less consistent, because it compares what investors pay for each euro of revenue rather than each euro of profit. For profitable businesses, a higher P/S ratio usually reflects higher expected growth or lower perceived risk, while a lower multiple can point to more muted expectations or higher uncertainty.

Continental currently trades on a P/S ratio of 0.68x. This sits close to the Auto Components industry average of 0.91x and the peer average of 0.65x, so on simple comparisons the pricing looks broadly in line with similar companies. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio for Continental of 0.29x, which is the P/S multiple suggested after factoring in the company’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and risk indicators.

This Fair Ratio is designed to be more tailored than a plain peer or industry comparison, because it adjusts for the specific characteristics of Continental rather than assuming all Auto Components stocks deserve the same multiple. Comparing the Fair Ratio of 0.29x with the current 0.68x suggests the shares trade above that modelled level, so on this metric the stock screens as overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Narratives bring this to life by letting you set a clear story for Continental, connect that story to concrete forecasts for revenues, earnings and margins, and see how that flows through to a Fair Value that you can compare with the current share price. Different investors might, for example, frame Continental around a more optimistic €100.0 Fair Value or a more cautious €58.58 view based on their own assumptions.

For Continental, however, we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Continental Narratives:

🐂 Continental Bull Case

Fair value in this bullish narrative: €72.79 per share.

At the recent price of €66.68, this narrative implies the shares trade at about 8.4% below its fair value estimate.

Revenue growth assumption in this narrative: 1.5% decline per year.

  • Focuses on higher margin automotive technologies, digital mobility solutions, and software, which are expected to improve the mix over time.

data.title: Investar Holding (ISTR) Rising Net Interest Margin Reinforces Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Investar Holding (ISTR) has just put fresh numbers on the table for Q1 2026, with recent quarters showing total revenue stepping from US$20.2 million in Q4 2024 to US$23.2 million in Q4 2025 and quarterly EPS ranging between about US$0.46 and US$0.64 over 2025. Over the same period, trailing twelve month EPS moved from US$2.06 to US$2.22 while revenue on this basis went from US$83.2 million to US$93.3 million, giving investors a clear line of sight on how the top line and per share earnings have been tracking into the latest release. Loan Book Grows While Credit Quality Shifts

  • Total loans moved from US$2,125.1 million in Q4 2024 to US$2,175.97 million by Q4 2025, while non performing loans over the same points rose from US$8.83 million to US$9.26 million.
  • What stands out for a bearish narrative focused on credit risk is that loan balances and non performing loans move together here, as total loans climbed roughly US$50.9 million between Q4 2024 and Q4 2025 while non performing loans also edged up, which fits concerns about concentration in certain loan types yet also shows that problem loans remain a small piece of a more than US$2.1b book.
    • Bears highlight that non performing loans increased from US$5.59 million in Q1 2025 to US$9.26 million by Q4 2025, and this data set confirms that credit quality did soften through the year even as the bank kept growing the loan book.
    • At the same time, trailing net income for the twelve months to Q4 2025 sits at US$21.85 million with a 23.4% net margin versus 24.3% a year earlier, so the earnings profile is still solidly profitable, which challenges the idea that rising non performing loans are already heavily eroding overall performance.
On the credit bears' side of the argument, rising non performing loans and a richer P/E make the risk case feel urgent, which is why skeptics often push readers to stress test that view in more detail through the 🐻 Investar Holding Bear Case.

Margins And Net Interest Income Still Supportive

  • Net interest margin across the reported quarters sits at 2.67% in Q3 2024, 2.87% in Q1 2025, 3.03% in Q2 2025, 3.16% in Q3 2025 and 3.07% on a trailing twelve month basis to Q4 2025, alongside a trailing net profit margin of 23.4% versus 24.3% in the prior year.
  • Supporters of a bullish view often point to this combination of mid single digit annual earnings growth and high margins, and the data backs parts of that story while also showing where it is being tested, since trailing EPS on a twelve month basis moved from US$2.06 in the year to Q4 2024 to US$2.22 in the year to Q4 2025 and one year earnings growth of 7.9% sits above the 5 year annual growth rate of 5.5%, even as net margin softened slightly from 24.3% to 23.4%.
    • For the bullish case, the fact that total revenue on a trailing twelve month basis increased from US$83.22 million to US$93.33 million while EPS also rose supports the idea that the bank is still turning top line into per share profit at a healthy rate.
    • However, the modest dip in trailing net margin alongside higher non performing loans shows that while profitability is strong in absolute terms, it is not moving higher in lockstep with revenue, which is an important nuance for anyone leaning heavily on the growth part of the bullish story.
For readers weighing this earnings profile against an upbeat long term story, it can be helpful to see how the full bullish case connects the margin picture with growth and valuation signals through the 🐂 Investar Holding Bull Case.

Premium P/E And DCF Gap Create A Mixed Valuation Signal

  • The shares trade at US$29.79 with a trailing P/E of 18.7x versus a peer average of 11.6x and US Banks industry average of 11.9x, while the supplied DCF fair value is US$50.64 and the trailing dividend yield is 1.48%.

This asks investors to weigh a richer multiple and recent shareholder dilution against multi year EPS growth of 5.5% a year and a one year earnings growth figure of 7.9%.

    • Supporters counter that trailing EPS of US$2.22, a 23.4% net margin and the current price sitting well below the DCF fair value keep the valuation debate open, especially with a 1.48% dividend yield adding some income on top.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to the present. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in today’s terms.

data.title: If You Believe In Robotics And Automation Stocks Then These Three Trajectories Could Define The Next Cycle
data.description:
Robotics is moving from science project to real-world workhorse just as investors are juggling inflation worries, oil-price swings, and changing interest rate expectations across major economies. While bond yields, energy markets, and central bank signals keep shifting, the physical AI push is quietly about one thing you can understand very clearly: productivity. This screener focuses on robotics companies that are not just building hardware, but are structured to translate automation demand into disciplined growth without stretching their balance sheets. Ahead, you will see 3 stocks from this Robotics and Automation screener that stand out for closer research.

ON Semiconductor (ON)

Overview: ON Semiconductor supplies intelligent power and sensing chips that sit inside electric vehicles, industrial automation, AI data centers, and factory robotics, helping customers manage energy efficiently and capture high quality data from the physical world.

Operations: ON generates most of its roughly US$5.99b in revenue from the Power Solutions Group at about US$2.81b and the Analog & Mixed-Signal Group at about US$2.26b, with the Intelligent Sensing Group contributing about US$0.93b.

Market Cap: US$32.65b

Investors looking at the robotics and automation theme may find ON Semiconductor interesting because it sits at the intersection of power electronics for EVs and energy storage, and intelligent sensing for factory automation and safety systems. The company is reshaping its portfolio toward higher margin silicon carbide and image sensing products, supported by programs like its Fab Right efficiency push and a sizeable share buyback plan. At the same time, recent results show margin pressure, one off losses near US$980m, and soft demand that has weighed on near term earnings, so execution on this transition really matters. Recent analyst upgrades and new design wins in energy storage suggest the story is evolving, but the full risk reward picture is more complex than a simple growth headline.

Silicon carbide ambitions and image sensing exposure are only half the picture. The real question is how that shift, the Fab Right push, and the buyback stack up in the 1 key reward and 3 important warning signs

Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC)

Overview: Lattice Semiconductor designs low power, small to mid range FPGA chips and supporting software that customers can program for tasks like industrial automation, robotics, AI at the edge, networking, and automotive systems across global markets.

Operations: Lattice generates about US$523.3m in revenue primarily from its Core Lattice segment, with sales spread across Greater China, the Americas, Europe, Japan, and other parts of Asia.

Market Cap: US$16.02b

Lattice can be relevant if you are looking at robotics and automation because its low power FPGAs and software suites slot into AI servers, factory equipment, and edge devices where flexibility and energy efficiency matter. Recent design wins in data centers, robotics, and security indicate deepening customer ties. At the same time, the company is priced for strong growth, current net margins are thin after a one off loss, and competition in lower and mid range FPGAs is intense, especially with regulatory and China exposure in the mix. If you want to understand how that mix of high expectations, edge AI and industrial demand, and rising security credentials could develop from here, you are only seeing the surface.

Growth expectations, thin margins, and intense FPGA competition make context vital. See how industrial, data center, and edge AI demand all stack up in the analysis report for Lattice Semiconductor

Teradyne (TER)

Overview: Teradyne designs automated test systems for semiconductors and electronics, and supplies collaborative robots and autonomous mobile robots that help manufacturers and logistics operators automate complex, repetitive tasks.

Operations: Teradyne generates most of its roughly US$3.19b in revenue from Semiconductor Test at about US$2.52b, with the Product Test segment contributing about US$358m and Robotics about US$308m.

Market Cap: US$59.55b

Teradyne sits at the intersection of AI, testing, and factory automation, with AI accelerator and data center demand feeding into its semiconductor and production board test systems, and its robotics arm targeting real world automation in manufacturing and logistics. Recent acquisitions such as Quantifi Photonics and TestInsight, plus the planned MultiLane joint venture, are aimed squarely at complex AI and high speed data center testing. A sizeable share buyback and higher dividend indicate management confidence in cash generation. At the same time, a high P/E, reliance on external funding, and pressure in robotics revenue and margins mean execution quality is important, particularly as tariffs and trade policies affect customer spending and visibility.

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess CGI (TSX:GIB.A) After Its Recent Share Price Strength?
data.description:

Approach 1: CGI Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today using a required rate of return.

For CGI, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on CA$ cash flows. The latest twelve month free cash flow is CA$2.06b. Analyst and model projections suggest free cash flow of CA$2.18b in 2026, rising to CA$2.32b in 2028, with further years extrapolated by Simply Wall St through to 2035. Each of these future figures is discounted back to a present value, then combined with a terminal value to arrive at an estimated intrinsic value per share.

This DCF output points to a fair value of CA$173.05 per share, compared with the recent share price of CA$105.07. On this basis, the stock screens as 39.3% undervalued using this particular model and set of assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: CGI Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of current earnings, which makes it a straightforward cross check against the DCF result you just saw.

What counts as a “normal” P/E usually reflects how the market views a company’s growth outlook and risk, with higher growth and lower perceived risk often going together with a higher multiple and the reverse also being true.

CGI currently trades on a P/E of 13.4x. That sits below the IT industry average of 19.3x and far below the broader peer group average of 69.1x, so on simple comparisons the shares look cheaper than many alternatives in the sector.

US$55.7m trailing losses keep profitability in focus

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Nano-X Imaging reports US$12.3 million in revenue against a net loss of US$55.7 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.88, so the business is still firmly in loss making territory despite quarterly EPS staying in a narrow band around a US$0.21 to US$0.23 loss.
  • Bears point to the scale and persistence of these losses as a core concern, and the current numbers give them plenty to work with:
    • Across the last six reported quarters, net losses each period sit around US$13 million to US$15 million while quarterly revenue sits around US$2.7 million to US$3.4 million, which aligns with the cautious view that high operating costs and a relatively small revenue base could keep margins under pressure.
    • Quarterly data also shows that even as revenue reached US$3.4 million in Q3 2025, net income excluding extra items was still a US$13.7 million loss, which echoes bearish concerns about the gap between commercial traction and the cost of scaling imaging hardware, AI solutions and teleradiology services.
Over several quarters like these, skeptics argue the path from US$55.7 million in trailing losses to any sustained profit is longer and riskier than bulls suggest, so it can be useful to see their full case in one place 🐻 Nano-X Imaging Bear Case.

Revenue creeping higher while losses narrow only slowly

  • Looking at the last six quarters, revenue has moved in a fairly tight band from US$2.7 million in Q2 2024 to US$3.4 million in Q3 2025 while quarterly net losses excluding extra items sit between about US$13.2 million and US$14.7 million, so losses remain many times larger than the revenue base even as sales edge up.
  • Consensus narrative highlights the push into new geographies and product lines, and the current mix of revenue and losses gives a mixed read on that story:
    • Quarterly revenue around US$3 million lines up with the idea that commercial activity is building, including imaging systems, AI solutions and teleradiology, yet the fact that net losses are more than US$13 million per quarter supports the view that high R&D and go to market spending continue to outweigh early sales.
    • With trailing twelve month revenue at US$12.3 million versus a US$55.7 million net loss, the figures fit the consensus concern that progress on installations and partnerships has not yet translated into margins that move meaningfully toward breakeven.

High growth forecasts sit beside premium P/S valuation

  • Forecasts built into the analysis point to revenue growth of about 45.4% per year and earnings growth of about 72.48% per year with profitability expected within three years, while the current trailing P/S multiple of 12.2x sits well above the US healthcare industry average of 1.2x and peer average of 1.6x, so expectations embedded in the price are already very ambitious relative to current US$12.3 million revenue and US$55.7 million losses.
  • Bulls argue that commercial ramp and AI driven recurring revenue can justify this kind of premium, and the figures lay out both the appeal and the stretch in that view:
    • The bullish narrative, for example, works off scenarios where revenue growth runs around 80.4% per year over three years with margins moving from deeply negative levels toward positive territory, which is a very different world from the current trailing EPS loss of US$0.88 and net loss of US$55.7 million.
    • At the same time, the high current P/S of 12.2x relative to industry and peers fits bullish claims that the market already prices in strong growth, so any gap between the forecast path to profitability and what actually shows up in future revenue and EPS will matter a lot for how the stock trades from a valuation angle.
